message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Seroprevalence studies provide an accurate measure of SARS-CoV-2 spread at a population level and the number of undiagnosed individuals. Repeated cross-sectional sero-studies are encouraged to monitor the spread of the virus. The aim of this study is to assess the seroprevalence rate among a random sample of Palestinians residing in the West Bank region of Palestine.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This second round cross-sectional study involved 1451 participants, who agreed to be interviewed and answer the questionnaire, where 910 of them agreed to participate in the sero- study and donate a blood sample to be tested for antibodies. The sample was randomly selected from the adult population, 18 years or older, living the West Bank region of Palestine.</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Serological tests for 910 adequate serum samples were done using immunoassays for detection of antibodies against SARS-CoV-2. Sociodemographic information and medical history data was collected.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Study findings indicate that as of October 2021, a seroprevalence rate of 75.9% (30% due to infection with Covid-19 virus and 45.9% due to vaccination), 95% CI (73.1-78.7). The results indicate that the prevalence of antibodies among those who are unvaccinated and undiagnosed was 45.2% with 95% CI (39.9-50.5%).</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>The average age of participants was 37.6 years old. 49.2% were females and 50.8% were males. In relation to COVID-19, 13.6% of respondents reported getting infected by Covid-19 with statistically significant difference (P_value=0.001) between males (10.7%) and females (16.5%).</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>. In terms of vaccination, 52.8% of respondents reported getting vaccinated with an important difference between males (64.3%) and females (40.9%), (P-value=0.000).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Our findings reveal a drastic rise in seroprevalence of SARS-CoV-2 antibodies due to infection and vaccination. This information is useful for assessing the degree of herd immunity among the adult population and provides better understanding of the pandemic. Population-based seroprevalence studies should be conducted periodically to monitor the SARS-CoV-2 seroprevalence in Palestine and inform policymakers about the efficacy of the surveillance system and the public compliance with vaccination policies especially among females</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Seroprevalence of SARS-CoV-2 Antibodies among vaccinated and non-vaccinated adults in the West Bank: Results of a repeated cross-sectional study
